data.title: StoneCo: Valuation Is Still Attractive
data.description:
StoneCo (NASDAQ:STNE) is a Brazilian fintech operator with its core business in payment acquiring, supported by banking services and software. In mid-March, the company reported 4Q 2024 earnings, which beat analysts’ estimates on a profitability basis. Management also provided several insights into its thinking on the divestiture of the software segment and the return of excess capital. All in all, strong earnings and managements’ continued adherence to its long-term 2027 guidance led to an appreciation in the stock from its lows. However, the upswing in stock performance didn’t last long. The arrival of new U.S. global tariffs put pressure on all stocks across the board. As is often the case, global investors are selling first and asking questions latter. This erratic behavior creates opportunities to buy companies that are not directly impacted by global tariffs. I believe that STNE could be one of them. Last time I wrote about the company, the stock was trading at $9.27, and I argued that its valuation presented an attractive entry point. This time, I’ve updated my valuation based on management hints provided during the most recent earnings call, and I’ve reached a similar conclusion, that STNE’s fair value should be materially higher than today’s market price.

Business model well protected from U.S. tariffs

The fact that StoneCo is a solely Brazilian company with its operational currency in Brazilian Reals protects it from most of the tariffs threats imposed by the U.S. Brazil faces a 10% tariff on all goods exported to the U.S., which is among the lowest rates applicable to America’s trading partners. On the other hand, Mexico – a major market for competitors such as Mercado Pago and NU Holdings, faces 25% tariffs on most goods, including steel, aluminum and automobiles. The impact of tariffs on the macroeconomy is mainly pro-inflationary. Subsequently, higher inflation leads to higher interest rates environment, which makes prepayment of receivables more expensive and, thus, less affordable. Both PagSeguro or StoneCo assume that SELIC rate should approach 15% by the end of 2025 from the current 14.25%. This gives the company sufficient buffer in case of further inflationary pressures. Paradoxically, StoneCo’s lack of regional diversification is, in this case, an advantage.

Additionally, the company’s focus on small and medium-sized businesses, particularly in non-discretionary sectors, such as gas stations, retail, drug stores, food, and horizontal software, provides good visibility into the economics of its customers. Since most of StoneCo’s clients are locally oriented businesses with no exposure to international trade, the impact from a U.S. - led trade war should be limited.

Breaking down StoneCo’s business into its key components, the company’s revenues consist of gross merchant discount rate (MDR) and income from prepayment of receivables. MDR is a fee that merchants pay to the company on debit and credit card transactions for processing payments. On the other hand, StoneCo pays an interchange fee to the card issuers, mostly banks, and card scheme fees to the network operators. Prepayment of receivables is StoneCo’s core product, called the working capital solution, where merchants can opt for faster access to their receivables for a small interest charge. StoneCo has a leeway in how it prices its products. After a long period of intense competition, management sees a more reasonable pricing environment, with no players undercutting on price.

Valuation

In the last earnings call, management provided several important hints on how to approach the company’s valuation. Firstly, they stated that StoneCo has excess capital amounting to BRL 3.0 billion. This capital will either be used for future growth projects or returned to shareholders if no better opportunities arise. Since StoneCo’s core business is relatively mature with a stable market share, I assume that the company will eventually return this capital to shareholders. The main reason for this assumption is the consistently declining number of new client additions, which indicates a saturated market. In 4Q, the company added only 156.7 thousand new clients, nearly 50 thousand fewer per quarter than the two-year average of 206 thousand. A similar trend is evident at its largest competitor, PagSeguro (PAGS), which added fewer than 100 thousand clients on a quarter-over-quarter basis.

Secondly, management offered some guidance on the valuation of the software business it plans to divest. The expected value from the sale should reflect the segment’s equity value plus the value of long-term agreements. It is hard to quantify the value of long-term agreements, but the equity of the software segment stands at BRL 4.0 billion. This figure can be used as a floor for valuation of the software segment. Since the software division contributed only 8% of total adjusted earnings before taxes (EBT), nearly all of StoneCo’s value comes from its payments and banking business. For that reason, I believe divesting the software division is a strategically correct decision.

The final piece of the valuation puzzle involves applying the correct cost of equity. The company has tradable bonds in U.S. dollars with a yield to maturity of 7.64%. Using a historical equity risk premium of 4.5% and adjusting for the inflation differential between Brazil and the U.S., the cost of equity in Brazilian Real terms is approximately 15%. For long-term growth, we can use management’s own estimate of 5%, mentioned in the impairment test in the notes to the financial statements. (Note 11.4)

By capitalizing earnings from the financial services segment using the spread between the cost of equity and the growth rate, we arrive at an implied market value of the company. Adding back the excess capital and the book value of the software division, we conclude with an implied share price of USD 15.4, more than 40% above the current market price.

Risks to my investment thesis

The number one risk to this investment thesis is the global macroeconomic environment. Although StoneCo is relatively well insulated from a global trade war, in a market-wide selloff, StoneCo would not be an exception. Another significant risk is the potential inability to divest its software division in a timely manner, which could undermine management’s credibility. Currently, the software division consumes management’s time and resources without delivering clear benefits to the company or its shareholders. Thus, a sale appears to be the only reasonable solution. A final risk is the potential reemergence of high inflationary pressures in Brazil, which could lead to increased financing rates for company’s products. That said, I believe that patient investors stand to benefit from the current mispricing, and that within a reasonable timeframe, the market price should converge with the company’s fair value.

data.title: Selling Micron? You May Regret It (Technical Analysis, Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Intro

Since my hold rating back in mid-January, Micron Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MU) has been crushed, as losses since then have totalled nearly 35%. I believe selling now would be a mistake. In the below analysis, the technicals are shown to be still highly bearish and so further weakness is probable. However, the fundamental setup of the stock has become highly attractive. Most recent earnings show that the company's financials continue to be strong and while Q3 guidance showed some deceleration in growth, resilience is expected moving forward. For the valuation, the P/S ratio has fallen off a cliff as pessimism has been extreme. I believe the contraction has been overdone and makes Micron's valuation attractive at current levels. Overall, technical weakness could for sure continue into the near future, but that won't stop me from upgrading the stock to a buy rating as now is already a great opportunity to get into a top AI beneficiary.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

Micron has been in a slow longer-term downtrend since early November and this trend line was important resistance back in mid-March. With the stock now far below, this trend line is not overly relevant at this point. The three nearest sources of resistance are identified above. The first is at around 75. This was the second of two gap downs early this month and this level may act as the resistance as the stock quickly retreated after a breakout two trading days ago. The next potential source of resistance could be the high 80s area. This was the level of the first gap down this month, but also note that this area was a floor for the stock from late January to early March. Lastly, there could be resistance in the mid-90s. This level was support back in November while being resistant in early February. With the stock still near fresh six month lows, there are no support levels in this timeframe.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA has been below the 200-day SMA for the past few months to show long term bearish sentiment. The gap between the SMAs has continued to expand and so momentum has been increasing. For the EMAs, there was a bearish crossover between the 9 day and 21 day EMAs back in late March, after a brief bullish period. The gap between the EMAs seems to be continuing its expansion and so bearish momentum is also accelerating for the near term. The 9 day EMA may have contributed to resistance just days ago.

The Bollinger Band's midline did not act as much resistance from December to March even though the stock was in a downtrend and so the trend was not a particularly strong one. However, Micron now sits far beneath the midline and the Bands are in a volatility expansion phase, making the stock no longer oversold. For the MACD, there was a bearish crossover back in late March but the most recent three histogram bars have been bullish. This could signal that peak near term bearish momentum is over. The MACD is continuing to make fresh six month lows, however, and so the long-term bottom may not be in. For the RSI, it currently has a reading of 34.87 after bouncing back from a sub-30 reading, but the bears remain in strong control. Similar to the MACD, the RSI confirms the latest plunge in the stock with a major low set earlier this month. Lastly, for the stochastics there was a bullish crossover recently but the %K has now flattened out. It has failed to break far above the 20 level and now a bearish crossover may be imminent as the %D is quickly catching up. The near term rebound's momentum is therefore fading. The stochastics also made a six-month low recently, again confirming the recent declines in the stock.

Takeaway

As a whole, I believe the basic technicals are quite undesirable for Micron currently. The chart shows heavy resistance levels above the stock, the moving averages show increasing bearish momentum, while there are some mixed signals in the indicators. For the near term, while the MACD histogram and RSI show some improvement, the stalling of the stochastic's %K suggests that the bounce in the stock is weakening. For the long term, all three indicators confirm the recent plunge, and so the bottom is unlikely to be in at this point.

Fibonacci

Retracement

Using the early November high and the recent six-month low, the Fibonacci Retracement provides key targets and resistance levels on the above chart. The nearest significant level of 23.6% is slightly above 75 in the stock. Recall that the mid-70s was mentioned as a resistance level as a downside gap occurred there earlier this month. Therefore, this level should be a significant obstacle for Micron if it were to rally again from here. The next resistance level from this retracement would be at 38.2% and the low 80s for the stock. Interestingly, the major December plunge held just above this area.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

The stock has fallen far beneath the cloud to reflect general bearishness for Micron. The cloud itself actually remains slightly positive until the middle of April. However, moving into mid-May, the Leading Span A has plunged far beneath the Leading Span B and is continuing to fall and so significant resistance is expected ahead. The Conversion Line actually spent a very brief period above the baseline in late March but has since plunged far beneath it as a signal of strong near term bearish momentum. The deviation continues to grow between the two lines and so momentum is, in fact, still accelerating. Lastly, the Lagging Span sits far beneath the late February levels in the stock, confirming the weakness. There was a sell signal in late March in the Ichimoku Cloud. The stock had fallen beneath the bearish cloud, the Conversion Line then broke below the baseline, and the Lagging Span also slumped below the early February stock price.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Micron reported their FY2025 Q2 earnings on March 20th and showed strong results overall, in my view. The company was able to generate revenues of $8.05 billion, up from $5.82 billion in the year ago quarter. For adjusted EPS, they had impressive results as the figure grew 271.4% YoY to $1.56. Consensus expectations were exceeded by a healthy amount with revenue able to beat by $155.52 million while EPS beat by $0.14. Margins also showed major improvements, with adjusted gross margin increasing from 20% to 37.9% while adjusted operating margin rose from only 3.5% to 24.9% YoY. These are encouraging signs for the business. As you can see in the chart above, revenue growth has dipped QoQ, but it remains at an above average level. For cash from operations, they saw growth of 223.0% as the $3.94 billion in this figure is the highest level seen in the past three years. As a whole, Micron's Q2 results show that the company is continuing to experience fundamental strength as a result of AI.

Guidance

For their FY2025 Q3, Micron issued respectable guidance figures. They are expecting revenues to be $8.8 billion +/- $200 million. At the midpoint, this represents YoY growth of 29.2% over the year ago quarter's $6.81 billion in revenues. This does represent a moderate slowdown in growth. However, revenue growth is still expected to remain at around average levels of the past few years. For EPS, the company is expecting an adjusted figure of $1.57 +/- $0.10. This implies midpoint growth of 153.2% over the prior year period's $0.62. This is also a slowdown from 271.4% growth in their Q2 but overall, growth is projected to remain robust through their Q3. Lastly, Micron is expecting a gross margin of 36.5% +/- 1.0% which is a notable improvement over FY2024 Q3's 28.1% figure. As a whole, some growth slowdown is not surprising as the business scales and so this guidance is enough to suggest that the fundamental outlook continues to be solid.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio is currently at very low levels compared to historic data. It now has a reading of only 2.439 after being over 7.5 in mid-calendar 2024. While some contraction is justified as revenue growth slows, the extreme contraction in the multiple seems to be an overreaction and is overdone, in my view. As I discussed earlier, revenue growth for their Q2 remains above average despite QoQ declines. Furthermore, Q3 guidance does show a moderate further slowdown, but growth is still expected to remain at the multiyear average. While this is the case, the P/S ratio has fallen far below its average to quite near three-year lows, and so there is a substantial disconnect between the valuation and the reality of the fundamentals. There is too much pessimism in the stock, making it undervalued. Currently, the Information Technology sector's median P/S ratio is at 2.51 and so Micron is at a 2.8% discount to peers. Given that growth estimates remain at healthy levels and that the company holds a leadership position in HBM for AI Infrastructure, I believe such a discount is unjustified as I expect fundamental outperformance for Micron.

Conclusion

There is no question that technical weakness is abundant, and it's likely to continue into the near future, as indications are still mainly bearish. However, selling now would be a big mistake in my view, as I believe the recent selloff is a prime opportunity to get into one of the top AI plays out there. Most recent earnings showed strong financial results, and while Q3 guidance may be a bit disappointing, the fundamentals are overall expected to remain solid moving forward. While revenue growth is expected to moderate, as per their guidance, it is still projected to hold at around average levels. This makes the major contraction in the P/S ratio seem like an overreaction. Furthermore, the stock's discount versus the IT sector seems unjustified based on the fundamentals and the company's strategic position in AI. Therefore, I upgrade Micron to a buy rating on high fundamental attractiveness, even though further technical losses may occur.

data.title: Weaponizing Tariffs: Best Stocks To Buy For A Recession
data.description:
Tariffs: Are we heading for a recession?

Reward and punishment have been the name of the game, as tit-for-tat tariffs have riddled headlines and impacted global markets. Despite Trump’s 90-day pause on tariffs, global markets moved lower following a historic rally on April 9, rebounding from bear market territory. On April 9th, the S&P 500 notched +9.52%, its greatest reversal since the 2008 Great Financial Crisis, as the Nasdaq rallied +12.16%, its best day since 2001, and the Dow Jones was up nearly 8%.

However, markets quickly retracted their gains as China and the U.S. imposed triple-digit tariffs on one another. There will be many days ahead where the news cycle and sentiment will impact the direction of the markets. The US Administration advised that the cumulative tariff on Chinese-produced imports will total 145%, and China increased its previous 84% rate to 125% on US goods. Following the move, China’s Finance Ministry stated, "The U.S. imposition of abnormally high tariffs on China seriously violates international and economic trade rules, basic economic laws and common sense and is completely unilateral bullying and coercion." Recent JPMorgan recession odds have increased by nearly 80%, with the Russell 2000 pricing in a 79% chance of economic downturn; Goldman Sachs forecast a 65% probability of recession in the next 12 months. And although “boys will be boys,” said the White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt, the weaponization of tariffs has led to mixed global reactions and risks.

A Number of Asset Classes Are Pointing To A Contraction

As recession fears increase, small-cap stocks, bonds, and base metals highlight an increased probability of contraction. The US Dollar Index (DXY) has fallen and US Treasuries are signaling a worsening economic outlook, experiencing their worst selloff since 2019 on April 11.

Big market swings indicate that emotions are high, as indicated in the fear and greed index below. Investors must be cautious, and consider removing emotion from the investment process when portfolio losses prompt knee-jerk reactions.

Historically, the market’s best days typically occur within two weeks of the 10 worst days. According to JPMorgan Asset Management Research, the impact of being out of the market during times of tumult can be costly for investors.

As displayed in the chart above, the market’s best days closely follow the worst, with seven of the 10 best days occurring within two weeks of the 10 worst days. JPMorgan further highlights:

“Emotional reactions to market declines can harm portfolios since the best days are likely to occur close to the worst days. Missing the 10 best days of the market over the past 20 years would have reduced a portfolio's annualized return by almost 50%, missing the top 40 days would have a negative annualized return on the original investment.”

This article offers investors insight on how to invest during periods of turmoil and three stocks based on Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings in preparing for a potential recession.

How to Invest During a Recession

The market has been moving toward bear territory given the expectation of a recession from the fallout of Trump tariffs and the resulting trade war with China. Bear markets tend to be gradual, rather than immediate. Many of the best days for Wall Street have occurred just before or during bear markets, or right after its end. For example, in the period surrounding the Great Recession, the Dow Jones began rebounding on March 9, 2009, +20% from its 7924.56 low during the bear market of 2007-2009. The S&P 500 was down 51.9% during that same period and surged 30% by mid-May 2009. Amid the COVID-19 pandemic, the markets experienced an atypical, month-long bear market rally in 2020 – the shortest bear market ever achieved and exited.

Typically, bear markets involve several months of downward pressure on equity markets offset by bonds. But as we’re seeing, bonds are selling off in conjunction with equities amid trade wars and uncertainty of the impacts on economic growth, and persisting inflation. Violent swings in US government debt can undermine investor confidence in the economy. So, navigating the current environment is key. Remembering your long-term investment objectives, regardless of the market cycle can help drive results. While some asset managers recommend sidelining cash, waiting for the opportune time to buy can be difficult. It’s about time in the market, not timing the market. As highlighted in JPMorgan's impact of being out of the market chart above, having no exposure typically is a recipe for disaster and a missed opportunity. One would be wise to focus on quality stocks during bear markets and times of recession; it goes beyond value and dividend production. I’ve said it many times: Focus on strong balance sheets, low debt levels, strong free cash flow, and overall strong fundamentals. Our Seeking Alpha factor grades instantly identify stocks that possess these important traits. With the anticipation of a recession, consider how best to respond to periods of downturn by removing emotion from the investment process. There are many sectors and investments you can consider to aid you in your investment goals, including data-driven ideas that may help recession-proof your portfolio.

Bear Market or Recession: Top Investment Ideas

Investors are shifting to defensive sectors. Some of the big market winners of the past, like the Magnificent Seven, have lagged in 2025.

While some rotations tend to be head fakes, the demand for safety amid high volatility has allowed the Consumer Staples sector (XLP) to advance as the top-performing YTD.

The demand for essentials like food and drink and household and personal care products makes consumer staples resilient and an opportunity to prepare for market swings and declines—and they’re resilient amid inflationary pressures. Where consumer prices rise, stock prices often follow, providing a strong likelihood and probability of success in pushing increasing costs to consumers without a significant drop-off in sales and revenues.

The Consumer Staples sector is the only positive sector YTD, up only 0.94%, unlike the Consumer Discretionary sector (XLY), which is down more than 16% YTD. That is why I have selected three of the best quant-rated staples to invest in for a bear market or recession.

1. Vital Farms, Inc. (VITL)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.46B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 29 out of 181

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 6 out of 54

Why are eggs so expensive right now?

Egg inflation persists – a combination of bird flu, tariffs, supply chain impacts, and stubborn inflation. Although retail egg prices have fallen since December, consumers are still paying record amounts, an average of $6.227 per dozen in March, according to the US Bureau of Labor Statistics.

Although egg prices hit new records in March, consumer staples benefit from passing costs onto consumers. With Easter, the “Super Bowl of eggs,” around the corner, demand is anticipated to raise egg prices further, benefiting Vital Farms Inc.

One of Morgan Stanley’s top packaged food picks, Vital Farms is expected to offer “a compelling ~20% three-year topline CAGR, driven by shifting consumer trends toward specialty eggs and egg products, distribution expansion, and near-term tailwinds from narrowing price gaps. Morgan Stanley assigned a price target of $40 to VITL.”

VITL currently trades under $35 per share and is a leading producer of ethically sourced, packaged, and distributed eggs, butter, and other products throughout the United States. Partnering with over 375 farms, the Quant Buy-rated egg supplier has gained as consumers flock to safe-haven investments.

VITL has faced some of the headwinds attributed to the bird flu, egg shortages, and pricing. Additionally, shares of Vital Farms slipped 8% following its Q4 2024 results, which indicated a ‘material weakness’ in revenue recognition. However, management has advised that they are focused on greater internal controls over their financial reporting going forward and continue to have a strong growth outlook, rallying +30% over the last year.

VITL Stock Growth & Profitability

Vital Farms has capitalized on strong consumer demand that resulted in a Q4 2024 revenue increase of 22% and a net income rise of 47%. The positive results led to VITL increasing FY2025 guidance by 5% above the consensus estimate which initially drove shares up 10% upon announcement.

Exceeding a $600M net revenue milestone in Q4, VITL stated its on track to meeting its $1B sales target by 2027. In addition to 28% Revenue Growth YoY and strong overall growth metrics, VITL is profitable, as showcased by its Profitability Grade. Return on Total Capital (TTM) of 16% vs. 7.39% for the sector, and nearly 15% Return on Total Assets (TTM) highlight some of its areas of strength. Additionally, VITL has a Cash Per Share (TTM) of 3.42 versus 1.81, an 89% difference.

Analysts remain positive about the stock, which has received five upward Wall Street Revisions in the last 90 days and only one downward revision.

VITL Stock Valuation

Up 32% in the last year, VITL has outperformed many of its sector peers, as inflation weighs on earnings power and the company passes on increasing costs to consumers. While VITL trades at a bit of a premium, as highlighted by a D valuation grade, its trailing PEG GAAP of 0.28x vs. 0.94 indicates some discount for this stock, which could offer egg-cellent growth potential.

2. Pilgrim’s Pride Corporation (PPC)

  • Market Capitalization: $12.22B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 4 out of 181

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 2 out of 54

If you didn’t think VITL is all that it’s cracked up to be, Pilgrim’s Pride Corporation is a meat packaging and food company focused on fresh and frozen chicken, pork, and other meat products. Distributing an array of brand offerings globally, PPC’s diversified portfolio and efficiencies have resulted in strong sales and operational excellence.

PPC is committed to diversification, which includes types of chicken, geography, and offerings like organic and air chill to drive profitable growth. Chicken is one of the fastest-growing proteins (+50%), so it offers a relative value compared to other proteins. PPC is taking advantage of favorable trends while trading at an extreme discount.

PPC Valuation & Momentum

Possessing an overall ‘A’ valuation grade, Pilgrim’s Pride has a forward P/E ratio of 9.91x, more than 36% below its sector average. In addition, its forward PEG of 0.21x is -91% difference from the sector, and forward EV/Sales of 0.75x indicates this stock is undervalued.

PPC’s momentum showcases strong price performance, beating sector median peers quarterly. Like the tailwinds affecting other food stocks, PPC is benefiting from strong demand and elevated meat prices being passed on to consumers. PPC is a leading global food company and the second-largest chicken producer in the world, so increasing demand for chicken versus red meat has increased PPC’s demand, aiding its growth and profitability figures.

PPC Growth and Profitability

Since 2021, Pilgrim’s Pride has experienced significant growth and a higher selling price due to people switching from red meat to chicken. PPC has capitalized on chicken being less expensive than other proteins, which includes feed conversion costs for the animal, quick and easy meal offerings, and growing appeal. Despite bird flu, weather impacts, and agricultural headwinds for PPC, sales growth has been strong. PPC has consecutively topped earnings, with Q4 2024 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.35 beating by $0.19.

Along with stellar growth, which includes year-over-year EBITDA Growth of 112%, forward EBIT Growth of 35% vs. the sector’s 5.5%, and an incredible forward ROE Growth of 21%, PPC is focused on unlocking shareholder value. Pilgrim Pride doubled its Q4 profits on a per-share basis. Pilgrim’s President, Fabio Sandri, noted:

“While we experienced a positive market environment with lower input costs and strong chicken demand in 2024, we elevated our performance across all regions through a continued focus on controlling what we can control…As such, we improved efficiencies through operational excellence, expanded relationships with key customers, and drove growth in our value-added portfolio.”

Given the market outlook, tariffs, and persisting inflationary concerns, higher commodity prices coupled with demand should bode well for PPC’s outlook, similar to my final pick, Tyson Foods, Inc.

3. Tyson Foods, Inc. (TSN)

  • Market Capitalization: $21.42B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 3 out of 181

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/11/25): 1 out of 54

In Top Stocks to Take on Trump Tariffs, I highlighted Pilgrim Pride’s incredible momentum and how its price return over the last year has outperformed the S&P 500 and its largest domestic competitor, Tyson Foods. However, the popular packaged food and meats company ranked #1 in its industry has maintained a Quant Strong Buy rating for the better part of a year despite volatility.

Tyson Foods Stock Quant Ratings & Factor Grades (as of 4/11/25)

Known primarily for chicken, Tyson Foods has been a standout amid the flight to safety, offering 35 consecutive years of dividend payments, strong dividend safety, and a 3.33% forward dividend yield.

Benefitting from spiking prices and exceptional quarterly results driven by its chicken segment, which achieved its best Q1 adjusted operating income in eight years, TSN raised its FY2025 guidance, helping offset declines in its beef segment. CEO Donna King stated, "This marks our third consecutive quarter of year-over-year increases across key metrics, including sales, adjusted operating income, and adjusted earnings per share." In addition to growing Q1 2025 revenue, Tyson topped EPS of $1.14 by $0.26. For the same quarter, Tyson generated $1B in operating cash flow, as management focuses on improving operational efficiencies and customer partnerships for growth.

Tyson’s strong growth grades and profitability have allowed it to continue delivering shareholder value. Tyson trades at a discount, as showcased in a forward PEG of 0.76x, which suggests the stock is currently undervalued by over 67%. Its forward EV/Sales metric is a -65.18% difference to its peers. Tyson’s EPS long-term forward (3-5Y CAGR) is a 193% difference to the sector, and the company maintains eight upward revisions in the last 90 days, with EPS projected to grow 20% in FY2025.

As investors remain focused on their portfolios amid tariff tit-for-tat and market volatility, it may be wise to consider three recession-resilient consumer staple stocks that are capitalizing on price hikes, especially companies paying dividends.

Conclusion

When recession-proofing a portfolio and searching for investments that will stand the test of time, consider the three consumer staple stocks that possess capital appreciation potential and strong fundamentals: VITL, PPC, and TSN. All have been positive over the last year despite recent volatility and possess excellent outlooks and collective characteristics, with tailwinds for continued momentum, growth, and profitability.

While a recession is typically caused by some catalyst, the market has been moving toward bear territory given the expectation of a recession from the fallout of tariffs and the resulting trade war with China. Although we are not presently in a recession, it’s crucial to be on alert for market indicators that display warning signals and portfolio diversification strategies that can prepare investors for periods of volatility.

Elevated prices are likely to persist, and consumer staples are necessities that can help serve as a defense during periods of uncertainty. Our three stock picks possess solid fundamentals and valuations, and positive growth outlooks, and their Factor Grades and Quant Ratings can help you make tactical investment decisions that are vital, especially if you're picking stocks without changing your overall risk level. Finding knowledgeable investment resources is a great way to be a successful investor in volatile markets. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help you find stocks that achieve diversification into desired sectors you like, including Top Consumer Staple Stocks.

data.title: OSI Systems: Key Strategies Driving Long-Term Growth
data.description:
I am beginning coverage of electronics manufacturer, OSI Systems (NASDAQ:OSIS). The factors that sparked my interest in OSI Systems are its strong revenue and earnings growth combined with a reasonable valuation. I also liked that the company received earnings upgrades from its covering analysts for FY25 and FY26. These upgrades typically provide a boost to the stock as analysts are confident in OSI's ability to produce positive results.

OSI Systems Company Background

Since, I'm beginning coverage of OSI Systems, I'd like to provide some company background so that investors get a better understanding of the business. OSI Systems operates as a vertically integrated designer and manufacturer of electronic systems for various applications. The company's products cover the following areas: defense, homeland security, healthcare, and aerospace.

OSIS operates three main segments which the company refers to as divisions: Security, Optoelectronics/Manufacturing, and Healthcare.

The Security Division handles security/inspection systems such as transmission/backscatter x-ray interrogation, radiation monitoring, 3-D computed tomography, radiation monitoring, metal detection, optical inspection, millimeter wave imaging, and chemical trace detection. These systems are used in a variety of settings and include border crossings, airports, seaports, government/military installations, freight forwarding operations, correctional facilities, sports/concert venues, etc.

The Security Division comprises about 67% of total revenue.

The Optoelectronics & Manufacturing Division handles applications in the following areas: automotive, homeland security, construction, medical imaging/diagnostics, and aerospace/defense, biochemistry analysis, pharmaceutical, telecommunications, industrial automation, and nanotechnology. Some specific applications in these fields include: patient monitoring equipment, diagnostics/imaging, satellite navigation sensors, laser guided munition systems, range finders, measurement control, and more. This division also handles manufacturing services where OSIS provides product design/development, printed circuit board assembly, LCDs, supply chain management, production manufacturing services, and more.

This division comprises about 23% of OSI's total revenue.

The Healthcare Division provides the manufacturing and distribution of patient monitoring solutions for sub-acute, critical, and perioperative care areas of hospitals. This division also offers ECG devices, ambulatory blood pressure monitors and wired & wireless networks which yield timely patient information. The Healthcare Division comprises the remaining 10% of total revenue.

Growth Drivers

OSI Systems has a comprehensive growth strategy. One of their main strategies is having a focus on international operations. OSI's global operations have a competitive advantage with competitive labor rates, which results in lower manufacturing costs. The company's international offices provide sales, marketing, and the ability to repair systems by providing close-to-the-market services. OSI's global presence enables the company to reduce delivery times to customers.

While OSI Systems already has a global presence, the company always has an eye on entering new markets as a part of its growth strategy. This focus tends to be for electronic systems for security inspection, patient monitoring, and remote monitoring. The company plans to enter new markets by growing internally and through acquisitions.

The illustration above shows that OSI receives ongoing recurring revenue from repeat services. It also points out the company's strong double-digit growth in the backlog of orders. The growing backlog demonstrates the growing demand for OSI's offerings.

OSI's vertical integration structure allows for direct sourcing of raw materials and subcomponents, reduced manufacturing and delivery times, and lower costs due to access to competitive labor markets. The company's customers benefit from OSI's vertical integration as the company can be a one-stop shop for design, customization, product development, manufacturing, and mass production.

The company has been focusing on capitalizing on the increasing trend of screening goods entering ports and borders. OSI expects growth for cargo inspection systems to screen for contraband. OSI has plans to service, upgrade, and replace existing security screening systems. This includes screening shipping containers, truck, rail cars, air cargo shipments, etc. The company also provides the software-as-a-service [SaaS] platforms that drive these screening systems. The SaaS can also be integrated with vehicle license plate numbers, driver license numbers, and other databases & info to enhance security efforts.

Another strategy is to improve existing medical tech such as patient monitoring systems, cardiology, remote clinical monitoring, connected care solutions, and related products. OSIS is focused on ensuring that its medical tech provides its customers with reliable, accurate, and cost-effective care.

OSI Systems always has its eye out for potential acquisitions as a part of is ongoing strategy. Acquisitions can help the company grow in new markets, to lower manufacturing costs, and to expand OSI's technical expertise.

Ajay Mehra took over as the new CEO on January 1, 2025. Mehra previously served as OSI's Chief Financial Officer and most recently as the President of the Security Division and Executive Vice President. The Security Division became a leader in its market sector, growing to over $1 billion in annual revenue under Mehra's leadership. This was accomplished by ensuring that OSI met the global security inspection needs by offering leading technology and innovative products. Ajay Mehra can be a key driver of growth for OSI with his knowledge of the company's business with a keen eye on continued innovation.

Recent Work Orders

OSI Systems has been getting a steady amount of new work orders to drive revenue. The company received a $12 million award at the end of January 2025. This contract is to provide multiple units of the Eagle M60 ZBx multi-energy mobile cargo and vehicle inspection system. These systems are used for international border monitoring/security. The contract includes maintenance and support for these systems.

The company received a $32 million order in February 2025. This order is to provide RF-based critical communications systems that are used for long-range secure communications. The name of the customer was not disclosed.

In March, the company received a $76 million order to provide airport screening for a major international airport. This includes OSI's RTT 110 explosive detection system for screening hold baggage. It also includes Itemizer 5X which is used for detecting trace amounts of explosives and narcotics.

In April 2025, OSI Systems received a $17 million order to provide cargo and vehicle inspection systems from a strategic North American customer. The order includes OSI's Eagle M60 cargo/vehicle inspection systems and related service & support.

The company also received a $4 million award in April for patient monitoring solutions and related supplies to a U.S.-based customer. This is a part of the company's Healthcare Division (Spacelabs Healthcare).

These recent examples of awards and work orders demonstrate the steady flow of demand that OSI is experiencing. I expect OSI Systems to continue to get a steady flow of orders since the company offers key products that are in demand in all three of its divisions.

Valuation

OSIS is trading at 18x expected EPS of $10.19 for FY26. This is higher than the Electronic Components industry's forward PE of 16.7x. The industry is expected to grow earnings at 13.8% over the next five years. This is higher than OSI's estimated earnings growth of 12.7% over the same period. With OSI's forward PE higher than the industry's average, and the company's growth lower than the industry, it is possible that the stock underperforms.

OSI Systems is expected to achieve EPS of $9.27 for FY25 which ends in June 2025 (consensus). This would be about 14% EPS growth. The expected EPS of $10.19 for FY26 would be about 10% growth over FY25. The higher expected growth for FY25 is due to higher expected revenue growth of 10%. Revenue growth for FY26 is expected to be about 5%. Announcements of new awards could increase the expectations for FY26. OSI could help achieve or exceed its earnings expectations with increases in margins from its strategy to drive manufacturing costs lower.

It is important to note that OSI's expected bottom line growth is higher than the S&P 500's (SPY) expected earnings growth of 11.3% for calendar year 2025 according to analysts. OSI's forward PE is also lower than the S&P 500's forward PE of 24x. So, OSI's stock has the potential to outperform the broader market over the next year or so.

Of course, OSI's stock performance depends on investor sentiment. It is possible that the stock still performs well if the company reports mostly positive news over the next year. This could be meeting or exceeding earnings expectations for most quarters. It could also be announcements of lucrative awards which could increase future earnings estimates.

Technical Perspective

OSI's weekly chart above shows the volatility at the end of 2024 and the beginning of 2025. It has been a wild ride. The good news is that the purple RSI line (middle of the chart) appears to be holding above the 50 level, which is bullish. However, the MACD (bottom of the chart) is currently bearish as the blue line remains below the red signal line.

The next earnings report, scheduled for April 24, 2025, is the next likely catalyst for the company and the stock. Consensus estimates are calling for revenue of $436.49 million and normalized EPS of $2.40 for Q3 FY25. OSI beat consensus estimates for both revenue and earnings in the past 5 quarters. So, we'll have to see if this trend continues. If OSI beats estimates and provides positive guidance, it could be a strong driver for the stock to move higher.

The Risks for OSI Systems

Since OSI Systems is relied upon for effective security systems, the company could be held liable in the case that the products did not properly detect explosives, weapons, etc. that it was designed to. The company could be held liable for losses as a result of a breach of its systems. A high-profile case involving a failed security breach could have a significant negative impact on OSI's reputation and lead to the loss of future revenue.

The company sells its products to various domestic and international governments and to large hospital networks. The loss of one or more of these customers could have a significant negative effect on OSI's revenue and earnings. The loss of one large customer could cause other customers to question OSI's offerings and lead to the loss of additional customers.

The industry is highly competitive. OSI System's competitors could provide more attractive offerings which could lead to the loss of market share. At the very least, the high competition could also result in OSI struggling to increase market share in the United States or in foreign markets.

Long-Term Outlook for OSI Systems

Overall, OSI Systems appears to have the right strategies in place to drive revenue and earnings growth at a strong pace. Revenue growth can be driven by expanding into new regions, continuously improving products, and through acquisitions, which are all a part of OSI's strategies. Earnings growth can be driven by the vertical integration structure, which can drive down manufacturing costs.

OSI's strategy has the potential to drive the company to meet/exceed its revenue & earnings estimates for most quarters. The results for Q3 FY25 could bring OSI's track record to 6 consecutive quarters of exceeding its revenue and earnings estimates.

Investors should watch for OSI to receive additional lucrative awards, which could increase future estimates. Also, keep an eye on news of acquisitions which could provide add-on growth.

data.title: Eni: Demand Side Dampens Oil Outlook
data.description:
Eni S.p.A. (NYSE:E)(OTCPK:EIPAF) has some things going for it. Enilive is valued at 10 billion EUR, which is more than 20% of Eni's EV, 16% when accounting for associated market value of NCIs. EIP's investments into Plenitude value it at around 10 billion EUR as well, 9 billion EUR when accounting net for associated market values of NCIs. Since the EBIT produced by the residual businesses is around 20x of the EBIT currently produced by Plenitude and Enilive, one would look for a value case in the traditional businesses, which are also still seeing development and growing solidly. The EV/EBIT indeed is low for the residual businesses, not much above 2.3x accounting for NCIs, far below peers. Eni could be a leading pick even, but we do think there are pressures on the business primarily coming from the oil market, and the total end of ECOBONUS, which was an area of activity for Plenitude which can be paid to install panels on your house and provide associated financial solutions, is a headwind as well, though the company expects the renewable capacity and related generation to increase though with prices still pressured. We also wonder about biofuel capacity risks due to the low incremental costs associated with conversions of traditional refineries.

Results

There was a small amount of production increases YoY, mainly driven by the inorganic increases brought on by the investments into Neptune Energy last year, which as we covered previously was adding around 10% on a quarterly basis. At the end of 2025, another 10% boost to production (though just of LNG) can be expected from the Congo FLNG project coming online. They also made a 1.2 billion boe discovery in Indonesia and another major discovery in the Ivory Coast.

While Enilive and Plenitude hit annual profit targets, there were profit headwinds. On an adjusted basis, development in EBITDA was entirely flat YoY. Importantly, the recent KKR (KKR) investment valuing Enilive higher was announced recently.

The traditional E&P business was down on lower prices for both LNG, associated to the lower prices of electricity and gas, as well as the YoY declines in the prices of crude as of the Q4. Declines were less pronounced as of the full year, and declines in reserves in other assets also shrunk the positive impact in the Q4 from inorganic increases in production. We expect a relatively flat outlook in terms of organic hydrocarbon production at this point. In terms of price realization, for LNG, our comment is mainly in line with our recent coverage of utilities which are seeing lower electricity prices, which is that, together with crude, the concerns around global growth brought on by the tariff concerns constitutes an issue for commodity outlooks. In the case of crude there is the additional issue that after several delays, ostensibly on insufficient demand for oil, the OPEC+ supply cut phaseouts are coming into play and this is a perfect storm for oil where considerable volatility in global growth forecasts are making oil prices sensitive to those revisions in demand expectations.

Bottom Line

While the exemption of smartphones and computers from tariffs, including coming from China which is now under 125% levies from the US, is going to likely cause a jump in commodity prices as an important component of consumer baskets is saved from the purchasing power impact that tariffs can have, there are lingering concerns around the world monetary order and the implications that these uncertainties may have on consumer confidence, with reports of the consumer confidence in the US plunging on concerns around real purchasing power. The US consumer, being the world's consumer, is an important global bellwether. Oil is going to be a demand story, particularly as the market grapples with the assurance of more expansive oil supply from the loosening OPEC+ taps, as well as the large vintage of strategic oil assets that were commissioned in the past couple of years. Even just on the run-rate YoY differences in oil prices, it should be expected that the performance of the vertically integrated Eni oil business should decline, with run-cuts hitting midstream, and the lower demand hitting upstream, with consumer confidence issues hitting downstream.

The other general risks we want to highlight is that the ECOBONUS fiscal bonuses are going to be hitting Plenitude performance for a short while longer before the ECOBONUS phaseout in Italy is lapped, in addition to pressures on electricity prices that might impact PPAs. For Enilive, we just want to highlight that the development of incremental biofuel and renewable diesel capacity is very cheap for existing refiners. In fact, one of the reasons why diesel prices rocketed on the Ukraine war was that a lot of capacity had been converted into use for renewable diesel production. It's a regulated market that depends on blending requirements and has never been on the forefront of the green agenda, and we believe there is always a supply risk, particularly if oil markets and midstream go under pressure once again as they were before the Ukraine war. The lowest cost renewable fuel assets seem to be in the US, which means the Enilive capacity, with FIDs for Malaysia, Italy and South Korea, may also be somewhat disadvantaged, although there tends to be sufficient margin regardless as the industry is relatively nascent.

In terms of valuation, the residual businesses after Enilive and Plenitude are worth around 2.3x EV/EBIT including all the corporate costs in the residual businesses. The average multiple in vertically integrated oil is around 6-7x EV/EBIT. It's definitely cheap. But with oil supply growth assured at this stage, we have concerns about positioning in oil, though Eni may become one of our favored relative picks at this point.

data.title: The Global Semiconductor Chessboard: Tokyo Electron Is Playing It Smart
data.description:
Overview

Tokyo Electron Limited (OTCPK:TOELY)(OTCPK:TOELF) is a Japanese semiconductor company that specializes in the manufacturing, development, and sales of semiconductor equipment. The company is headquartered in Minato City, Japan. It is one of the largest suppliers of semiconductor production equipment (SPE), which contributes 90% of the revenue. Manufacturing of Flat Panel Display equipment, used for LCD and OLED displays, accounts for a much smaller revenue share.

Tokyo Electron operates above the value chain. The business prospects of these higher value chain firms depend on the market demand for semiconductors, and the market for the same is in a state of flux due to the recent tariff war launched by US President Donald Trump.

I have previously written about the trends in the semiconductor industry in my article on ASM International (OTCQX:ASMIY). In that article, I spoke about the seasonality in the semiconductor sector and the bullwhip effect experienced by upstream firms due to fluctuation in the demand for semiconductors. Given the current volatile market conditions, let's now try to identify the key headwinds and tailwinds impacting Tokyo Electron and the broader semiconductor industry.

Tailwinds

Increasing Business from China

Geography-wise analysis of revenue share reveals that there is significant dependence on China, and the share of China has consistently increased (barring 2022). Interestingly, in 2023, the company's dependence on the United States reduced, while its dependence on China reached an all-time high of 44%.

The reason for this rise needs to be analyzed further. In 2023, sales to the US reduced drastically from $344 billion to $168 billion. On the other hand, sales to China rapidly increased from $527 billion to $813 billion. One of the major reasons for this trend could be that China is trying to build its manufacturing muscle in semiconductors and, therefore, is working towards building its base in the downstream part of the semiconductor value chain. This has been in response to the increased export restrictions by the US government on semiconductors to China. This has not only impacted the dynamics of semiconductor demand in China, but also in the United States. With US semiconductor manufacturers facing a demand crunch, they have reduced their demand for SPEs as well, which has contracted the sales for Tokyo Electron from the US.

The graph below is crucial to highlight how China is building its semiconductor manufacturing muscle. It displays that imports of semiconductor for China have begun reducing, already driven by the increased domestic production. Now, with the Trump tariffs in place, China is likely to witness further drying up of semiconductors from the United States. This will only push them to build their manufacturing capacities further. With this push incoming, we expect the sales value from China to rise, and that will act as a tailwind for Tokyo Electron. This will only go on to increase the intrinsic shareholder value with rising free cash flows.

Recovery in Consumer Electronics Demand

The year 2024 witnessed a decline in demand for consumer electronics, which is also reflected in the sales trend of Tokyo Electron, as given below.

The sales value reduced from $15,447.6 million to $12,800.7 million. However, a recovery is expected in the upcoming period. Samsung expects demand for electronics to increase again. Similarly, TSMC expects the first half of the year to be sluggish, followed by a strong recovery in the second half of the year. Sony expects a record demand for its play stations. This optimism translates to greater business for Tokyo Electron because the business prospects of upstream firms depend on the optimism of the downstream firms because there is a lag between the production of semiconductor equipment and the final production of semiconductors to the production of electronics.

This is already apparent when we look at the market for Wafer Fab Equipment, which is expected to have significant growth at a CAGR of 5.6%. The Asia Pacific region is likely to be the epicenter for this growth, and Tokyo Electron is expected to derive significant advantage because of its positioning.

Partnership with IBM

Tokyo Electron and IBM recently decided to continue their joint research and development efforts. This partnership will be aimed towards advancing next-generation semiconductor nodes and advanced semiconductor technologies. The agreement is an extension of their 20-year partnership, which led to several breakthroughs in terms of semiconductor technologies and innovation. The new partnership will be focused on developing smaller nodes and chiplet architecture which will achieve both performance and energy efficiency. This is very much in line with the trend that the global semiconductor market is following, in response to the increased AI demand and the demand for smaller chips.

Valuation

In order to get an idea about the target price of Tokyo Electron, I have done a DCF valuation of Tokyo Electron based on estimates from Seeking Alpha and my own. I have assumed that since semiconductors is a growing industry, the company will be growing at 10% for the upcoming 5 years and at a slower rate of 5% thereafter, eventually decreasing to a terminal growth rate of 3%. I have done the WACC calculations separately based on Japan’s risk-free rate and equity risk premium. According to the valuation, Tokyo Electron is currently undervalued based on the current stock price of $68.56. The target stock price is $78.82, offering a potential upside of 14.96%. If the market takes this difference into account, we are likely to see a rise in the stock price in the coming period.

Risks

I have already discussed how Tokyo Electron is likely to receive significant business from China due to China’s move towards domestic production of semiconductor equipment. However, there lies a significant risk for Tokyo Electron. China is likely to make moves towards complete indigenization of semiconductor manufacturing, and domestic semiconductor production is just the first step in that regard. In the longer term, though, Tokyo Electron could see a significant challenge to its business prospects if it becomes overly dependent on China for its revenues. Currently, the company is able to ride on the back of China's semiconductor indigenization efforts because 90% of Tokyo Electron’s demand comes from SPEs which act as an input for semiconductor manufacturing.

However, in the longer run, if Tokyo Electron wants to survive, it has to look towards diversifying its revenue source based on product or geography. This is because it is not just China, but also other countries like India which are looking towards semiconductor production indigenization because semiconductor manufacturing is being increasingly seen as a source of power, especially because the production of defense equipment is dependent on semiconductors. Tokyo Electron has already been making moves to diversify geographically and its revenue share from Taiwanese and Korean markets have witnessed a rise of 5% and 3.4% respectively.

Conclusion

The semiconductor industry is currently in a state of flux and therefore, it becomes crucial for any company operating in this field to identify the right opportunities and invest as well as mitigate the risks.

The risks currently are significant, with the two largest economies -- in the US and China -- currently being locked in an economic war. One of the sectors significantly impacted is the semiconductor. With Tokyo Electron operating in the upstream part of the value chain, the bullwhip effect is likely to further amplify these fluctuations. However, even with these factors, Tokyo Electron is well positioned to benefit, especially if the company is able to reposition itself to derive maximum benefit from the situation.

With revenue incoming from China set to increase further, Tokyo Electron can expect further increase in its free cash flows. However, this also presents a risk for the longer term. If Tokyo Electron gets complacent about its revenue source from China, it could bring about a setback because China is set to move towards complete semiconductor production indigenization and that could reduce revenue for Tokyo Electron in the future.

Given the potential upside of 14.96% from my valuation exercise, my recommendation would be to buy the stock.
