data.title: Main Street Capital: You Wanted A Discount, Now You Got It
data.description:
Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) won the hearts of retail investors with highly attractive dividend payments and their monthly distribution. Since its IPO, MAIN delivered outstanding performance on a total return basis, with a significant portion of it distributed right to the shareholders' pockets.

The only weak spot it showcased during the last couple of quarters? The price. Numerous investors expressed their willingness to invest in MAIN, but at much lower prices than over $60 per share the Company reached at the beginning of 2025.

But now? The situation is much different. Since MAIN's ATH recorded in February at over $63 per share, its stock price declined by around 20%. Naturally, the chaotic and drastic approach of Trump's administration to global trade is one of the main reasons for the decline. As we all know, Trump flipped the global trading table at the beginning of April, igniting a potential trade world of the U.S. with the rest of the world. Currently, the situation is at pause with most countries, but "discussions" with China escalated to 125–145% tariffs between the U.S. and China.

All these events lead to one thing—uncertainty, which is a solid adversary for the broad stock market. Concerns regarding potential recession, increased inflation, weaker job market, and worse international trading conditions lead to uncertainty, which also includes MAIN.

Main Street Capital: Top-Tier BDC

BDC stands for a business development company (financing solution providers), which invests debt and/or equity in companies unable to acquire banking / public market financing.

MAIN is broadly considered a leading player in the field, and there are several reasons for it. Starting with its earlier-mentioned track record and outstanding performance, MAIN currently has investments in 190 companies through three portfolio segments (lower middle-market, private loans, and declining (purposefully) middle-market). As I mentioned in my previous article, MAIN has a reasonable dividend coverage even assuming more interest rate cuts. This BDC is quite resilient due to its relatively large equity position and meaningful exposure to fixed-rated debt investments.

On top of that, MAIN is unique in its structure, as it's an internally managed BDC, which provides several benefits over externally managed peers. What are some of these benefits? As MAIN comments itself:

Because we are internally managed, we do not pay any external investment advisory fees, but instead directly incur the operating costs associated with employing investment and portfolio management professionals. We believe that our internally managed structure provides us with a better alignment of interests between our management team and our employees and our shareholders and a beneficial operating expense structure when compared to other publicly traded and privately held investment firms which are externally managed, and our internally managed structure allows us the opportunity to leverage our noninterest operating expenses as we grow...

Does it end here? Not at all—MAIN also showcases high diversification (reducing overall portfolio risk) in terms of several factors: geography, portfolio company, industry, etc.

Regarding the risk of portfolio companies default? MAIN's non-accruals amounted to 0.9% at fair value and 3.5% at cost, which is a reasonable level, but... there's a "but". Should risk factors regarding the economy materialize, MAIN's portfolio companies will get hurt. That's inevitable. Also, those of them who generate meaningful business through international trade will be hit even harder. Therefore, investors may expect increasing non-accruals, especially given the length of the period US companies have operated (and still do) under the high interest rate environment.

Luckily, the majority of MAIN's debt investments are first-lien debt, which provide a priority in the repayment schedule in the case of potential default of a portfolio company.

Here's the Silver Lining

Uncertain, potentially hard times will create problems for businesses. Those businesses may find themselves requiring external capital to realize their strategy and uphold market positions. With the likelihood of further interest rate decreases, I believe this will all lead to a more active transactional market. As MAIN highlights itself, it has a strong liquidity position that should enable it to capitalize on the best opportunities available in the market:

Strong capitalization and liquidity position - stable, long-term debt and significant available liquidity to take advantage of opportunities

Also, let's remember we have Main Street Capital on the agenda today. The BDC, which proved its effectiveness during uncertain times. Take COVID-19 as an example, which led to the government freezing the economy for some periods. Yes, the stock market reaction was rapid and harsh (as expected from the market that doesn't like uncertainty), but uncertainty passed, the economic environment improved and MAIN's investors at that time realized outstanding results.

Now, I see (potentially the beginning of it) another opportunity to increase my position in MAIN. It's often the case that when investors see strong valuation increases, they cease investing with fear of "buying at the top" and they wait for declines. At the same time, the very same investors are often scared to capitalize on such declines and wait for a "rebound" to feel safer when investing. Isn't that funny? I decided to break this cycle and invest in MAIN.

Make no mistake, I had a position in MAIN before that and added a relatively small amount. I often spread my investments for several installments to "average" the investment during times of high volatility. With that said, MAIN is a "buy" for me.

data.title: Is JPMorgan A 'Buy' Following Its Q1 2025 Earnings?
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, JPMorgan (NYSE:JPM) is a quality bank within the global financial system given that the bank has good fundamentals and leading franchises across several segments of the industry. This is a position that is not easy to challenge, giving JPMorgan some sort of competitive advantage over peers over the long term.

However, as usual with companies that have a quality profile and a strong business profile compared to its closest competitors, JPMorgan’s valuation is not cheap, and historically, its shares have traded at a premium to peers. As shown in the next graph, while Wells Fargo (WFC) was the bank with the highest valuation multiple, measured by price-to-book ratio, during 2015-18, this changed following the bank’s cross-selling scandal and JPMorgan has traded consistently as the highest value large U.S. bank since then.

This is justified by the bank’s superior profitability, capitalization, and business growth in recent years, a trend that has been maintained over the past few quarters. Indeed, the bank has recently reported its Q1 2025 earnings, maintaining a very good operating performance despite increased market volatility and uncertainty about the macroeconomic environment. Indeed, its Corporate and Investment Banking (CIB) segment has reported a very strong operating performance, which was more than enough to offset some weakness elsewhere, namely regarding net interest income (NII) and higher provisions for credit losses.

In the first quarter of 2025, JPMorgan’s total revenues increased by 8% YoY to $45.3 billion, beating street estimates by close to 4%. However, NII and other revenues had a mixed performance given that rates have become a headwind for revenue growth following the Fed’s rate cuts over the past few months.

The central bank maintained its key rate unchanged until September of last year, but since then, has performed three rate cuts, which are negative for NII growth of the banking system, a trend that JPMorgan is obviously not immune to.

Nevertheless, its NII amounted to $23.4 billion in the last quarter, representing an annual increase of 0.9%, but a decline of 0.4% from the last quarter of 2024. This shows that JPMorgan’s NII has likely peaked at the last quarter of 2024, justified by lower rates and some deposits margin compression. Moreover, loan growth remained somewhat muted, as total loans increased by only 2% YoY and were flat compared to the previous quarter, not being enough to offset weakness from other factors. This also explains why its net interest margin (NIM) declined by 18 basis points (bps) in the quarter to 2.47%, being the lowest level since the third quarter of 2023.

Despite that, the bank raised its guidance for NII in 2025, expecting now to reach $94.5 billion, while previously was expecting $94 billion, a small difference that is justified by higher NII coming from markets operations compared to what it was expecting previously.

While NII was not a great support for revenue growth over the last quarter, its noninterest revenue performed quite well, supported mainly by a great performance from markets activities. Noninterest revenues amounted to $22.6 billion in Q1 2025, up by 17% YoY and 12% higher than reported in the previous quarter. Due to this strong performance in noninterest revenue, its revenue mix is now more balanced between NII and other revenues, even though NII is still the largest component accounting for 51% of total revenues.

By business segment, Commercial & Investment Bank had the best performance last quarter, reporting revenues of $19.7 billion, up by 12% YoY. Within this unit, Equities had a fantastic quarter with revenues reaching a new quarterly record of $3.8 billion (+48% YoY). This was justified by higher volatility on the capital markets that led to increased customer activity, related to stocks and derivatives, boding well for Q2 2025 revenues as market volatility has been quite high in recent weeks. Fixed income also had a good quarter, with revenues of $5.8 billion being up by 8% YoY, Investment Banking revenues of $2.27 billion were up by just 2% YoY and disappointed to some extent, as higher debt underwriting and advisory fees were offset by lower equity underwriting fees.

Asset & Wealth Management also had a good performance, even though it’s a smaller unit and has a lower impact on the bank’s overall top-line performance, reporting revenues of $5.7 billion in Q1 2025, up by 12% YoY, being supported by strong inflows and higher average market levels. Consumer & Community Banking reported revenues of $18.3 billion (+4% YoY), while the Corporate segment had revenues of $2.3 billion, also up by around 4% compared to the same period of last year.

Regarding costs, its total operating expenses amounted to $23.6 billion in Q1 2025, up by 3.5% YoY, driven by pressure on costs related to the inflationary environment, namely on wage growth. Nevertheless, this was a lower growth rate than revenue growth, which means the bank was able to improve its operating efficiency during the last quarter. Its cost-to-income ratio was 51% in Q1, being among the best in the U.S. banking system and a strong indicator that the bank has a solid business model and a good level of efficiency.

On the credit costs side, this was the main headwind for earnings growth in the last quarter, as net charge-offs and provisions increased considerably from Q1 2024. Its total credit costs were $3.3 billion in the last quarter, up by 74% YoY. This is justified by an increase of around $400 million in charge-offs (+20% YoY), mainly related to some credit deterioration on credit cards, and also due to a net reserve build of $973 million. This provision increase was justified by the bank using a weaker macroeconomic outlook, in which it reflected an increase of 30 bps in the unemployment rate due to economic uncertainty and elevated risks in recent weeks, leading to a reserve build of $441 million in the consumer segment and $549 million on wholesale. While the bank’s loan portfolio continues to perform well, JPMorgan decided to do a reserve build last quarter, which seems sensible considering the recent events related to Trump’s tariffs, which are expected to have a negative impact on economic activity ahead.

Despite higher operating expenses and credit costs, JPMorgan was able to report net income growth of nearly 11% compared to the first quarter of 2024, to $14.3 billion. However, investors should note that this result includes a one-off gain of $446 million related to the First Republic acquisition, thus adjusted for this effect, its ‘recurring’ net income would be around $13.8 billion, up by 7.4% YoY. Its return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability in the banking sector, was 18% in the last quarter, a very good level of profitability that is above-average compared to its peers.

Regarding its capitalization, JPMorgan also has a very good position given that its CET1 ratio was 15.4% at the end of last March, being also above the average of its closest peers. This shows that JPMorgan has a solid balance sheet and can distribute a large part of its earnings to shareholders, while maintaining at the same time a superior capitalization in the banking sector.

However, during the last quarter the bank reported a decline of 30 bps in its capital ratio due to capital returns and higher risk-weighted assets, a trend that is likely to be maintained in the short term as increased market volatility is likely to result on higher capital charges for the securities in its balance sheet. However, I don’t think this will be enough for the bank to change much of its capital return strategy ahead, returning most of its earnings to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks.

During Q1 2025, JPMorgan returned $11 billion of capital to shareholders, of which $7.1 billion were related to share buybacks and the rest to dividend payments, representing about 77% of its earnings. Showing that the bank is confident about its business outlook, it has recently increased its quarterly dividend by 12% to $1.40 per share, or $5.60 annually, but at its current share price this leads to a forward dividend yield of about 2.4%, which is not particularly attractive to income-oriented investors.

This means income is not a great reason to buy its shares, making potential upside potential the main reason to consider its shares. However, recent market volatility has made the outlook much more uncertain, which led to a share price correction of about 15% since its all-time high some weeks ago. While its price-to-book value ratio is now below 2x (vs. more than 2.4x book value at the peak), it’s still above its average over the past couple of years (around 1.8x book value). Thus, its shares don’t appear to be undervalued right now.

Conclusion

JPMorgan is a quality bank and has a very solid business, but recent market volatility and economic uncertainty has increased considerably in recent weeks, being a potential headwind for earnings growth over the coming quarters. While its valuation has declined in recent weeks due to the overall stock market weakness, I think the risk-reward profile is still biased to the downside and investors should wait for a larger pullback to enter a position on JPMorgan or to buy more of its share if they are already a shareholder.

data.title: Shutterstock: Don't Fear AI Too Much
data.description:
Today, I want to take a look at Shutterstock (NYSE:SSTK), and understand why I rate the company as a BUY. My thesis is that even though AI is taking over the image services market, as AI images still have to be paid for, to be used publicly, I expect Shutterstock to keep a large part of its revenue generating abilities through the sale of rights to their AI-generated images, and as in my eyes the company is currently trading extremely cheap, considering the recent stock price drop along with the overall market, I believe that my rating is justified. To understand my thesis further though, we will first go through the market overview, to then take a quick look at the Q4'24 earnings report, analyze the company's fundamentals through our 9 Pillar analysis, and finally calculate their intrinsic value through all four of our valuation models.

Analysis

Market Overview

First of all, I want to point out that currently, Shutterstock is trading at the lowest price it has ever traded at, in its lifetime as a public company.

To me, this, of course, makes some sense, as investors are firstly, afraid of the rise of AI-generated content, and secondly, the whole market is crushing, which is dragging Shutterstock down as well. On the other hand, just to put things into perspective, despite the stock price being the lowest it has ever been, the company's revenue is at all-time highs.

So despite the fears, facts speak for themselves, and the fact that Shutterstock is generating 100.00% more revenue than they did when they traded at around 22.00 a share, tells me that there probably is a lot of undervaluation going on right now.

Although this might be controversial, as through the long term, many investors believe that the company will lose a large portion of its revenue to the rising use of cheap AI-generated images, which I don't blame, as it might be entirely possible, let me bring some light to the situation.

To publicly use AI-generated images, you do have to have a licensing agreement with the company that provides you with the tools to generate them, as essentially, the AI image is their property. So what I am trying to say is that to use AI images, users still have to pay, which means that Shutterstock is keeping their revenue generating abilities.

You might think that the company provides only real images, but if that's the case, you are wrong. Shutterstock hasn't been falling behind the market at all; on the contrary, their AI image generating tool is one of the best I have used in a while, and of course, users have to pay to generate official images. Currently, the AI image-generating tool does account for only a very small portion of the company's revenue, but as the markets start shifting from real images to AI images, I believe that this revenue share will grow.

So in my eyes, things aren't looking bad at all; the company has been innovating, and is prepared for the changing markets. But now let's take a quick look at their recent advances.

Q4'24 Earnings Report

On the 25th of February, the company reported a miss on their Non-GAAP EPS by $0.18. The EPS was expected at $0.85 but came in at $0.67. It is a similar case with the revenue, which was reported as a miss by $3.86M, expected at $254.16M, but coming in at $250.30M. Though YOY, it is up by 15.20%, so in my eyes, despite the miss, it is still a great feat.

9 Pillar Analysis

But now, as I am a long-term value investor, instead of analyzing the company's short-term successes', let's take a look at their long-term fundamentals, and really understand how the business is doing.

First, though, let's get to understand the 9 Pillar Analysis. It is composed of 9 individual pillars, with Pillars 1 and 2 showing the 5-year P/E and P/FCF ratios. Pillars 3, 4, and 5 show the 5Y revenue, net income and FCF growth, respectively, with pillar 6 depicting the historical profitability and pillar 7 focusing on the company's share buybacks. Pillar 8 analyzes the company's dividend CAGR as well as yield and payout ratios, and Pillar 9 helps me understand whether a company generates enough cash flow to cover all its debt. Below you can see that Shutterstock gets a rating of 4/9, so let's zoom in closer and understand what is happening.

Financial Growth (Pillars 3, 4 and 5)

First of all, I want to have a look at how well Shutterstock has been growing their financials, starting with revenue. From 2020, the company's revenue increased from $667.00M to $935.00M in 2024, accounting for a CAGR of 7.13%. Below you can see how consistent the revenue growth has been.

On the other hand, the net income of the company has slipped heavily in 2024, moving from $71.77M in 2020, to $35.93M. Though looking at the graph below, you can see that from 2020 to 2023, it has actually increased by 53.64%, accounting for a 4Y CAGR of 18.58%. So in my eyes, 2024 is just a one-time decline in an otherwise growing environment.

Finally, regarding the FCF, that has been decreasing by quite a bit, from $137.59M in 2020, to -$14.57M in 2024, accounting for an adjusted CAGR of -10.53%. So overall, it looks like the core business has been doing quite decent, while the company's cash flow has been, to an extent, mismanaged.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

Now moving to the valuation, I first want to take a look at the PE ratio of the company. The 5Y PE is sitting quite high at 28.13, much higher than the current PE of only 14.41. Take into account that the current PE accounts for the 2024 net income, which, as we just saw, was really low compared to the 5Y average, and, in my eyes, will recover soon. Nevertheless, to show what I mean, If we were to compare the current price of the stock to the 5Y average net income sitting at $77.19M, our PE would be only 5.57, which is, in my eyes, extremely low.

Regarding the P/FCF, the 5Y average is sitting at 18.31, and as the FCF in 2024 has been negative, the current ratio is sitting at -35.49. Now if we were to perform the same thing as we just did with the PE, we would find that the price to the 5Y average FCF is currently only 5.20, again, in my eyes, extremely undervalued.

Debt (Pillar 9)

Before moving to calculating the intrinsic value, I still want to have a look at two more pillars, starting with Pillar 9, the debt. The company's LTL/5Y FCF - Dividends ratio is sitting at only 0.44, which means that with their average FCF, they would be able to pay off all their LTL in about 2 years, meaning that the company is in my eyes, completely safe if there was financial hardship to come. Through last year, they had to increase this debt by about 192.80%, from $57.00M to $166.00M.

Dividends (Pillar 8)

One of the main reasons behind this huge increase in debt was the company's negative FCF that year, which meant that they had to borrow money, to pay out dividends. In 2024, their FCF payout ratio sat at -290.87%, which wouldn't bother me that much if it was a one-time thing, but looking at the historical 5Y payout ratios, they have steadily been increasing, from 17.73% in 2020 to 40.32% in 2023. This is due to the company's dividend CAGR being really high, sitting at 15.36%, much higher than the YOY FCF growth. This CAGR is great for long-term dividend investors, but with the increasing payout ratio, the question is if, through the long term, it is sustainable. In my eyes, the company will have to very soon lower their dividend increases.

So now that we have gone through the 9 Pillars, it does seem like the company is trading extremely cheap compared to its historical net income and FCF. But let's figure out their Intrinsic value with certainty.

Intrinsic Valuation

To do this, we will first jump to the Graham's Formula revised, where I will plug in the 5Y EPS and combine it with my projected EPS CAGR of 4.00%. This will give us an intrinsic value of $19.24, 32.26% higher than the current trading price of $14.55.

For the second valuation, we will look to the DCF model, where I will predict the 10Y + FCF CAGR of only 3.00%, and with a high discount rate of 10.33%, get an intrinsic value of $29.65, showing that truly, even with low projected growth, the company is extremely undervalued.

For the third valuation model, I will compare Shutterstock to two of its peers: MediaAlpha, and Ziprecruiter. I will take these companies' PE ratios, and average them out to 20.29, which, when multiplied with the current EPS of Shutterstock of $1.01, will give me an intrinsic value of $20.50.

Finally, looking to the DDM, I will project a low dividend CAGR of 4.00% and still get an intrinsic value of $21.68.

Now looking to the Ultimate Valuation, we will average out the 4 values, and get an ultimate intrinsic value of $22.77, which is 56.46% higher than the current price of $14.55, supporting my BUY rating.

Conclusion

The reason why I don't rate Shutterstock as a STRONG BUY, despite their heavy undervaluation, is because there is, of course, risk coming from AI. No one truly knows what will happen or if the company will retain its customer base. What I do believe though is that with their current undervaluation and healthy fundamentals, at the current trading price, even if their growth heavily decreases over the next years, investing at the current trading price would still bring great returns.

data.title: Reddit: A Good Time To Buy The Sharp Dip (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
When markets are this volatile, it's the exact perfect time to be an active investor. I continue to individually assess every stock in my portfolio, as valuations are gyrating wildly and each company's impact from tariffs and the slowing macroeconomy will be different.

Reddit (NYSE:RDDT), in particular, is a stock that I think has tremendous rebound potential. The fast-growing social media platform, best known for its candid discussion forums, has seen its share price collapse ~40% since the start of January. For much of 2024, Reddit was trading at unbuyable levels from a valuation perspective: for investors who have held out patiently on the sidelines, this is the right dip to buy.

I was initially quite bullish on Reddish immediately after its IPO, but my outlook on the stock soured when its valuation multiples started climbing. I downgraded Reddit to a sell rating earlier this year in January, when the stock was just below $200 per share. Today, however, Reddit is hovering around the $100 mark, down ~50% from its peaks, while its user base continues to showcase extraordinary growth that no other social media platform can match. As such, I'm giving Reddit a two-notch upgrade to a buy rating.

Since valuation is the major tiebreaker in my decision to reinvest in Reddit, and it's the main thing that has changed in the Reddit story over the past few months, let's start with a discussion of Reddit's latest multiples. At current share prices near $100, Reddit trades at a market cap of $18.30 billion. After we net off the $1.84 billion of cash on Reddit's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $16.46 billion.

Reddit does not provide full-year guidance, but for Q1, the company is pointing to $360-$370 million in revenue, or 48-52% y/y growth; and $80-$90 million in adjusted EBITDA (a 23.3% midpoint margin that implies nearly 20 points of y/y margin expansion).

Meanwhile, Wall Street analysts have a consensus estimate of $1.74 billion in revenue for this year, or 37% y/y growth. This puts Reddit at a 9.4x EV/FY25 revenue multiple.

Yes, it's true that Reddit trades at a richer valuation multiple than the industry bellwether, Meta (META). But at the same time, Meta is seeing far more sluggish user growth trends, and the Street is only calling for low-teens growth for Meta in 2025 while Reddit is growing nearly 3x as quickly. I'd gladly pay a two-turn premium for Reddit now (and note that before the recent stock market crash that converged Reddit and Meta's multiples more closely, Reddit traded at more than double Meta's valuation, hence my prior skittishness on the stock).

The long-term bull case for Reddit

This reasonable valuation buys investors a strong list of long-term tailwinds. In my view, the top reasons to be bullish on Reddit are:

  • Far superior growth rates versus social media peers proves the differentiation of its brand. As just mentioned, Reddit is expected to grow at a ~3x pace versus Meta. In my view, the company has a wide moat versus other social media peers: Reddit focuses less on pictures, videos and personal promotion; and rather on discussion boards and topics of interest.
  • Unlocking international growth via machine translation into eight foreign languages. Reddit is deploying automatic machine translation to serve up English-language content to more Reddit users across the globe, currently available in eight languages. International language content is searchable on Google, and the faster the company is able to translate its content into other languages, the more organic traffic it can bring to its sites.
  • Diversifying monetization streams, especially with data licensing opportunity. Like most social media companies, Reddit generates the majority of its revenue from selling advertising space. But like X, the company has also started selling a data licensing feed as well as setting up a user e-commerce ecosystem to drive more revenue. The company cited a third-party study that noted that 40% of internet users seek advice and reviews on Reddit before purchasing a product: which makes Reddit discussion boards incredibly valuable data for marketers.
  • Profitable bones. Reddit is scaling tremendously, and it is already minting adjusted EBITDA in a 20-30% range. To me, the fact that revenue is growing north of 30% y/y and adjusted EBITDA is growing substantially faster is yet another reason why Reddit's relatively premium near-term valuation multiples are justifiable.

The risk investors have their eyes on: a hiccup in U.S. user growth?

With all of these positives being said, however, there is a reason Reddit stock has faltered this year beyond just the broader market turmoil, and investors should go into this stock with eyes wide open.

For social media companies, the core long-term driver for growth is user growth: but especially growth in the U.S., because (at least for now) U.S. users generate substantially more advertising revenue than users in other regions.

Reddit has never seen a sequential decline in users until Q4, when its U.S. user base dipped slightly to 48.0 million DAUs (daily active users).

The company cited a change in the Google search algorithm as the core reason why. On the Q4 earnings call, CEO Steve Huffman explained the situation as follows:

Late in Q4, we did experience some volatility from Google Search triggered by a periodic algorithm change but traffic from search has recovered so far in Q1 and we've regained momentum. What happened wasn't unusual, referrals from search fluctuate from time to time and they primarily affect logged-out users. Our teams have navigated many algorithm updates over the years and did an excellent job adapting to these latest changes effectively. This particular swing was interesting because, along with it, we saw a corresponding increase in the query term, Reddit which suggests users are searching with the specific intent of reaching Reddit and this propensity continues to rise.

We typically see two types of users on Reddit: those who scroll and those who seek. Scrollers engage with Reddit for its core community and conversation product, while seekers come to, or end up on, Reddit for answers to their questions. Just a few years ago, adding Reddit to the end of your search query felt novel. Today, it's a common way for people to find trusted information, recommendations and advice. Regardless of why or where a user starts their journey, they should have a seamless path to discovering what they need on Reddit."

The good news is that management says the company has regained momentum in Q1, so we hope to see Reddit return to sequential U.S. growth. Meanwhile, overall DAUs are still climbing, thanks to 4.7 million sequential adds in international markets - which accelerated from 4.3 million adds in Q3, and which we expect to continue picking up steam as machine translation continues to unlock more local-language content.

Another helpful offset to weaker U.S. user growth: ARPU continues to climb, both in the U.S. (+28% y/y) and internationally (+25% y/y), feeding Reddit's tremendous 71% y/y revenue growth in Q4 to $427.7 million, which beat Wall Street's expectations of just $405.5 million (+62% y/y).

Reddit's revenue is also climbing much faster than its cost base, leading to substantial margin accretion. As shown in the chart below, Reddit's revenue growth outpaced its cost growth by 3.5x, while adjusted EBITDA margins climbed 27 points y/y to 36.1%:

We have to underscore and emphasize that no other company in the social media space has anything close to these metrics. They also face similar issues, with stagnant or declining U.S. users. Snap (SNAP), for example, has seen flat DAUs in the U.S. for the entirety of FY24, while its revenue grew only double digits in Q4 (compared to Reddit's 72% y/y growth) and its adjusted EBITDA margin of 18% was half of Reddit's.

Q1 look-ahead

The next major catalyst for Reddit will be its Q1 earnings release, which will be announced on May 1.

As previously mentioned, the company is guiding to 48-52% y/y growth on the top line, with a 23.3% adjusted EBITDA margin that implies 19 points of y/y expansion at the midpoint versus just a 4.1% in the year-ago Q1:

Reddit is not a "set it and forget it" type of stock: the volatility that its stock has seen in Q1 is proof of that. There are several key items I'm watching for in Q1 that could change my stance on the stock:

  • U.S. user trends. Of course, this is the big-ticket item we need to assess critically. Management vaguely noted that it "regained momentum" in early Q1 after the company received less traffic inflow from Google search in Q4, but we need to see if the company can return to sequential growth in U.S. DAUs.
  • Is the profit guidance conservative? In the prior-year Q4 to Q1, adjusted EBITDA margins fell from 9.3% in Q4'23 to 4.1% in Q1'24. This year's guide implies a 36.1% margin in Q4 dropping to 23.3% in Q1. The thirteen-point decline seems steep: we should watch for the company's ability to exceed this forecast.

Key takeaways

All in all, however, I believe Reddit's sharp ~50% fall from peaks has rendered the stock incredibly attractive again. To me, Reddit's risks (both the company-specific U.S. user slowdown as well as general tariff-war risks, as a slowing macroeconomy may impact advertiser demand) are already priced into the stock, which is a great buy-the-dip candidate at <10x forward revenue.

In my view, investors will benefit from buying the stock ahead of the May 1 earnings date, where relief on U.S. user trends may help Reddit recover closer to prior heights.
